1643	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1644	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1645	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1646	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1647	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1648	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1649	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1650	
1651	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1652	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1653	\end{theorem}
1654	
1655	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1656	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1657	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1658	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1659	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1660	% by restricting to a non-empty
1661	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1662	\begin{proof}
1663	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1664	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1665	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1666	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1667	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1668	
1669	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1670	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1671	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1672	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1673	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1674	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1675	
1676	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1677	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1678	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1679	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1680	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1681	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1682	
1683	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1684	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1685	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1686	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1687	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1688	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1689	
1690	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1691	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1692	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1693	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1694	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1695	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1696	\end{proof}
1697	
1698	\section{Mathlib status}
1699	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1700	
1701	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1702	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1703	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1704	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1705	generic-flatness argument consumes
1706	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1707	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1708	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1709	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1710	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1711	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1712	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1713	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1714	are introduced in
1715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
83	
84	\begin{lemma}\leanok
85	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
86	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
89	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
90	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
91	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
92	\end{lemma}
93	
94	\begin{proof}
95	
96	
97	
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
101	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
102	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
103	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
104	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
105	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
106	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
107	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
108	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
109	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
110	\end{proof}
111	
112	\begin{lemma}\leanok
113	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
114	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
116	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
117	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
118	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
119	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
120	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
121	  isomorphism.
122	\end{lemma}
123	
124	\begin{proof}
125	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
166	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
167	their proof sketches.
168	
169	\begin{lemma}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
173	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
176	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
178	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
179	  \[
180	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
181	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
182	  \]
183	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
184	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
185	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	
191	
192	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
193	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
194	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
195	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
196	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
197	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
198	\end{proof}
199	
200	\begin{lemma}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
204	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
207	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
208	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
209	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
210	  \[
211	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
212	    \;\cong\;
213	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
214	  \]
215	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
216	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
217	  of \(N\).
218	\end{lemma}
219	
220	\begin{proof}
221	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
222	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
223	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
224	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
225	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
226	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
227	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
228	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
229	  equation of
230	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
231	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
232	\end{proof}
233	
234	\begin{lemma}
235	
236	\leanok
237	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
238	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
240	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
241	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
242	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
245	    \;\cong\;
246	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
247	  \]
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
252	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
253	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
254	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
255	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
256	\end{proof}
257	
258	\begin{lemma}
259	
260	\leanok
261	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
262	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
264	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
265	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
266	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
267	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
268	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
269	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
270	  \[
271	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
272	    \;\cong\;
273	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
274	  \]
275	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
276	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
277	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
278	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
279	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
280	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
281	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
282	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
283	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
284	\end{lemma}
285	
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
288	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
289	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
290	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
291	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
292	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
293	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
294	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
295	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
296	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
297	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
298	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
299	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
300	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
301	
302	  \medskip
303	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
304	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
305	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
306	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
307	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
308	  \(U \mapsto
309	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
310	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
311	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
312	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
313	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
314	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
315	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
316	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
317	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
318	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
319	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
320	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
321	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
322	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
323	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
324	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
325	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
326	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
327	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
328	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
329	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
330	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
331	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
332	\end{proof}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	
336	\leanok
337	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
338	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
340	  Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf
341	  restriction map
342	  \[
343	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
344	  \]
345	  read as a map of \(R'\)-modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) as the
346	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). This is
347	  the \(R'\)-side localization fact transported in movement~(3) of the proof of
348	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
349	\end{lemma}
350	
351	\begin{proof}
352	
353

2314	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2315	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2316	
2317	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2318	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2319	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2320	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2321	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2322	
2323	\begin{definition}
2324	
2325	\leanok
2326	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2327	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2329	  % NOTE: The definition carries no \uses: the Lean construction is
2330	  % `IdealSheafData.ofIdeals (fun U => Module.annihilator Γ(X,U) Γ(F,U))`, which is well-defined
2331	  % unconditionally and does NOT depend on the localization bridges at definition time. The two
2332	  % facts lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map and lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen are needed
2333	  % only for the downstream CHARACTERIZATION (the reverse inclusion / equality on affine opens, the
2334	  % "annihilator forward direction"), which is not yet a blueprint block.
2335	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2336	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2337	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2338	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2339	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2340	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2341	  % Polynomials" (read from
2342	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2343	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2344	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2345	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2346	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2347	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2348	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2349	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2350	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2351	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2352	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2353	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2354	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2355	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2356	  \[
2357	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2358	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2359	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2360	  \]
2361	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2362	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2363	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2364	
2365	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2366	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2367	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2368	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2369	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2370	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2372	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2373	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2374	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2375	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2376	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2377	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2378	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2379	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2380	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2381	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2382	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2383	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2384	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2385	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2386	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2387	
2388	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2389	\end{definition}
2390	
2391	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2392	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2393	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2395	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
2396	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
2397	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2398	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2399	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2400	  \(U\):
2401	  \[
2402	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2403	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2404	  \]
2405	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2406	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2407	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2408	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2409	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2410	\end{lemma}
2411	
2412	\begin{lemma}
2413	[Annihilator ideal sheaf agrees with the module annihilator on affine opens]
2414	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal}
2415	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal}
2416	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator, lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le,
2417	    lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2418	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
2419	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) of finite type, and \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section
2420	  over \(U\) of the annihilator ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2421	  (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) \emph{equals} the module-annihilator of the sections:
2422	  \[
2423	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2424	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2425	  \]
2426	  The inclusion \(\subseteq\) is \cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}; the reverse
2427	  inclusion is the content here, now available since
2428	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} is proved.
2429	\end{lemma}
2430	\begin{proof}
2431	  \uses{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le, lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map,
2432	    lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2433	  Only the reverse inclusion remains. By construction
2434	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) is \(\mathrm{IdealSheafData.ofIdeals}\) of the local data
2435	  \(V \mapsto \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)}(\mathcal{F}(V))\), and its section over \(U\)
2436	  is the infimum over the basic opens \(D(f) \subseteq U\) of the comaps of the local
2437	  annihilators. For \(a \in \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) one must show its
2438	  restriction lands in \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) for every
2439	  \(f\). Quasi-coherence and finite type give, via
2440	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}, that
2441	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f)) = (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\mathcal{F}(U)\) over
2442	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\); then
2443	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} identifies the localized annihilator with the
2444	  extension of \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\), into which the restriction
2445	  of \(a\) lands. Hence \(a \in \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\), giving the reverse inclusion.
2446	\end{proof}
2447	
2448	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2449	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2450	  localization]
2451	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2452	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2453	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2454	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2455	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2456	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2457	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2458	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2459	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2460	  \[
2461	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2462	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2463	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2464	  \]
2465	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2466	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2467	
2468	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2469	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2470	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2471	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2472	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2473	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2474	\end{lemma}
2475	
2476	\begin{proof}
2477	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2478	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2479	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2480	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2481	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2482	
2483	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2484	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2485	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2486	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2487	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2488	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2489	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2490	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2491	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2492	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2493	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2494	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2495	\end{proof}
2496	
2497	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2498	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
2500	  \mathlibok
2501	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2502	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
2503	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
2504	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2505	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
2506	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
2507	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
2508	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
2509	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
2510	\end{lemma}
2511	
2512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2513	[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
2514	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
2516	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization,
2517	    lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
2518	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
2519	    lem:qcoh_pullback_fromSpec, lem:modules_restrict_linear,
2520	    lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear, lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence,
2521	    lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general, lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_hP1}
2522	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2523	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2524	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2525	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2526	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2527	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2528	  %  associated to $M$.
2529	  %  [...]
2530	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2531	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2532	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2533	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2534	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2535	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2536	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2537	  \begin{enumerate}
2538	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2539	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2540	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2541	    \item the section-restriction map
2542	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2543	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2544	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2545	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2546	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2547	  \end{enumerate}
2548	\end{lemma}
2549	
2550	\begin{proof}
2551	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization,
2552	    lem:qcoh_pullback_fromSpec, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
2553	    lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general, lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence,
2554	    lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear, def:gamma_image_ring_equiv,
2555	    lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_hP1}
2556	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2557	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2558	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2559	
2560	  Part (2) is the \emph{gap-2 transport}. Because \(U\) is affine, Mathlib's
2561	  canonical affine immersion \(\mathrm{fromSpec} : \Spec \Gamma(X, U) \to X\) has
2562	  image \(U\) and identifies \(U\) with the spectrum of its ring of sections. We
2563	  obtain (2) by instantiating the general-ambient-scheme transport core
2564	  \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general} at the affine open immersion
2565	  \(j := \mathrm{fromSpec}\), then bridging its conclusion to the basic-open
2566	  restriction.

2569	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,
2570	  \mathcal{M}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\). This is produced in two steps: first the
2571	  pullback \((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{M}\) is
2572	  again quasi-coherent by \cref{lem:qcoh_pullback_fromSpec} (quasi-coherence is
2573	  preserved under pullback along \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\)); then, since this pullback
2574	  lives on the affine scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\),
2575	  \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} turns that quasi-coherence into the
2576	  required isomorphism of the \(\widetilde{(-)}\)-counit.
2577	
2578	  \emph{Applying the core.} Feeding this P1 datum, the element \(f\), and the slice
2579	  element \(f' := \sigma^{-1}(f)\) --- where
2580	  \(\sigma = (\Gamma\Spec\text{-iso})^{-1} \circ
2581	  \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}\,\top\)
2582	  (\cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}) is the \(\top\)-level section ring iso, so the
2583	  transport hypothesis \(\sigma(f') = f\) holds by construction --- to
2584	  \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general} yields that the section
2585	  restriction
2586	  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top) \to
2587	  \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,D(f'))\) is
2588	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(\Gamma(X, U)\).

2591	  statement, posed on the image opens \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top\) and
2592	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,D(f')\), with
2593	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for the consumer-facing
2594	  basic-open restriction \(\mathrm{restrictBasicOpenₗ}\)
2595	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear}). The image opens are
2596	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top = U\) and
2597	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,D(f') = D(f)\). The one nontrivial coherence is that,
2598	  under the identification \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top = U\), the section ring
2599	  iso \(\sigma\) restricts to the identity --- precisely \(\rho(\sigma\,f') = f\),

3200	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \eta_M(m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). Equivalently,
3201	  this conjugated map is the \emph{inverse} of the regrouping isomorphism
3202	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv},
3203	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\theta) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
3204	  = \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\), and in particular an isomorphism with \emph{no}
3205	  flatness hypothesis.
3206	\end{lemma}

3208	\begin{proof}
3209	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read, lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read,
3210	        lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}
3211	  The section identity is an immediate consequence of the \(g^*\)-transpose crux
3212	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}, with the domain and codomain pinned by
3213	  the domain read Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} (\(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\)) and
3214	  the codomain read Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} (\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)).
3215	  By definition the base-change map (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is the
3216	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-adjunction transpose of the inner pushforward comparison
3217	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) (with \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\)); by the counit form of the
3218	  hom-set adjunction it factors as \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the counit
3219	  \(\varepsilon_g\), with no opaque transpose remaining,
3220	  \(\Gamma(\theta) = \Gamma\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\).
3221	  Substituting this factorization, the conjugated map
3222	  \[
3223	    \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \circ \Gamma(\theta) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}
3224	      = \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \circ
3225	        \Gamma\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma(\varepsilon_g) \circ
3226	        \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}
3227	  \]
3228	  is exactly the left-hand side of the section-level equation of
3229	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}, which states it equals
3230	  \(\operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\). On the generator \(r' \otimes m\) the conjugated map
3231	  therefore returns \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m\), the value of the inverse regrouping
3232	  isomorphism Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}; both maps are \(R'\)-linear and
3233	  agree on generators \(1 \otimes m\), so they coincide. This is the ``boils down to the
3234	  equality'' \((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M\) of the source, with no
3235	  flatness hypothesis used anywhere.
3236	\end{proof}

3322	% `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
3323	% "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
3324	% L927--938).
3325	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
3326	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
3327	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
3328	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
3329	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
3330	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the

3332	% $$

3335	% as $R'$-modules."
3336	\begin{proof}
3337	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map,
3338	        lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
3339	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read,
3340	        lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
3341	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso,
3342	        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
3343	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
3344	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
3345	
3346	  The proof is a direct module computation: we unfold the \emph{definition} of
3347	  \(\alpha = \mathtt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\) and evaluate each adjunction
3348	  unit/counit through the single-step tilde dictionaries, never invoking the
3349	  composite-mate recognition. By
3350	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, \(\alpha\) is the
3351	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-adjunct of the map
3352	  \[
3353	    \beta : f_*\widetilde M \longrightarrow g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M
3354	          = f_*\,(g')_*(g')^*\widetilde M
3355	  \]
3356	  obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
3357	  \(\eta : \widetilde M \to (g')_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) of the
3358	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the square's commutativity
3359	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Explicitly, as adjunct,
3360	  \(\alpha = \varepsilon_{f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M} \circ g^*(\beta)\), where
3361	  \(\varepsilon\) is the counit of \(g^* \dashv g_*\). We trace each factor on global
3362	  sections over the affine model, on a generator \(r' \otimes m \in R' \otimes_R M\).
3363	
3364	  \emph{Step 1 — the inner unit \(\eta\) is the extension-of-scalars unit.}
3365	  On \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the projection \(g'\) is the
3366	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map of the algebra inclusion \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\)
3367	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}). By the pullback dictionary
3368	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, \((g')^*\widetilde M\) is the tilde of the
3369	  extension of scalars \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\), and that lemma identifies the
3370	  adjunction unit with the algebraic unit of extension of scalars; hence on sections
3371	  \(\eta\) is the \(A\)-linear map
3372	  \[
3373	    M \longrightarrow (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M, \qquad
3374	    m \longmapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m .
3375	  \]
3376	  This is forced by the universal property of the tensor product and the algebra map
3377	  \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\); no mate identity is used.
3378	
3379	  \emph{Step 2 — apply \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\).} The pushforward
3380	  dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} reads \(f_*\) of a tilde as
3381	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Restriction of scalars does not move
3382	  elements, so on sections over \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) the map
3383	  \(\beta = f_*(\eta)\) (with codomain rewritten by \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\)) is the
3384	  same assignment \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), now read \(R\)-linearly into
3385	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
3386	
3387	  \emph{Step 3 — apply \(g^* = R' \otimes_R (-)\).} The pullback dictionary
3388	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} for \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\) reads
3389	  \(g^*\) of a tilde as extension of scalars \(R' \otimes_R (-)\) along \(\psi\), and is
3390	  functorial: \(g^*(\beta)\) on sections over \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) is
3391	  \(\operatorname{id}_{R'} \otimes \beta\),
3392	  \[
3393	    R' \otimes_R M \longrightarrow R' \otimes_R \bigl((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\bigr),
3394	    \qquad
3395	    r' \otimes m \longmapsto r' \otimes \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr).
3396	  \]
3397	  The domain \(R' \otimes_R M\) here is exactly \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\)'s reading of
3398	  \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde M))\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}).
3399	
3400	  \emph{Step 4 — the counit \(\varepsilon\) is the extension-of-scalars counit.}
3401	  Writing \(W = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) for the target \(R'\)-module, the counit
3402	  \(\varepsilon : g^* g_* \widetilde W \to \widetilde W\) of \(g^* \dashv g_*\) is, on
3403	  sections over \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and again by
3404	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} (which identifies the counit with the
3405	  algebraic action map of extension of scalars), the \(R'\)-linear map
3406	  \[
3407	    R' \otimes_R \bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\psi W\bigr) \longrightarrow W,
3408	    \qquad r' \otimes w \longmapsto r' \cdot w ,
3409	  \]
3410	  where \(r' \cdot w\) is the \(R'\)-action on \(W\). The codomain \(W\) is exactly
3411	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)'s reading of \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M)\)
3412	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}).
3413	
3414	  \emph{Composition.} Composing Steps 3 and 4, the conjugated section map sends
3415	  \[
3416	    r' \otimes m
3417	    \;\overset{g^*(\beta)}{\longmapsto}\;
3418	    r' \otimes \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
3419	    \;\overset{\varepsilon}{\longmapsto}\;
3420	    r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
3421	    = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m ,
3422	  \]
3423	  the last equality because the \(R'\)-action on \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\)
3424	  multiplies into the \(R'\) tensor slot: \(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m)
3425	  = ((r' \cdot 1) \otimes 1) \otimes m = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). Thus
3426	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \ggg \Gamma(\alpha) \ggg \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) is the
3427	  \(R'\)-linear map \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\), which is
3428	  precisely \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\) of
3429	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
3430	  (\(\mathtt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)). No flatness hypothesis is used, and the
3431	  value falls out of the tensor-product universal property together with the algebra
3432	  map \(\varphi : R \to A\) — not from any conjugate/mate coherence identity.
3433

3439	  % `base_change_mate_domain_read` / `base_change_mate_codomain_read`. No flatness
3440	  % hypothesis and no appeal to `base_change_mate_gstar_transpose`.
3441	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}
77

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
137	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
138	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
139	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
140	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
141	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
142	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
143	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
144	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
145	\end{definition}
146	
147	\begin{definition}
148	
149	\leanok
150	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
151	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
153	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
154	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
155	  % affine patches" (read from
156	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
159	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
160	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
161	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
162	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
163	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
164	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
165	  \[
166	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
167	  \]
168	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
169	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
170	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
171	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
172	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\begin{definition}
176	
177	\leanok
178	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
179	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
181	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
182	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
183	  % affine patches" (read from
184	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
185	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
186	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
187	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
188	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}

190	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
191	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
192	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
193	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
194	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
195	\end{definition}
196

198	
199	\leanok
200	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of

7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30

32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  The structure morphism is the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); it is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}

2361	\end{lemma}
2362	\begin{proof}
2363	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value,
2364	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor,
2365	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp,
2366	        def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
2367	  The concrete identity is the instantiation of the abstract variable-legs reindex
2368	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs} at the literal pullback projection legs
2369	  \(g' = \operatorname{pr}_1\), \(f' = \operatorname{pr}_2\): with the codomain read
2370	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} being definitionally the variable-legs read at
2371	  those legs, the concrete statement follows directly. The remainder of this proof records the
2372	  mathematical content of that abstract identity.
2373	
2374	  Throughout, let \(e : X' \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Spec}(A \otimes_R R')\) be the
2375	  canonical isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackSpecIso_mathlib}, and
2376	  \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\), \(\iota_{R'} : R' \to A \otimes_R R'\) the tensor
2377	  inclusions, so that by Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor} the two projection legs
2378	  factor as
2379	  \[
2380	    g' \;=\; e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A,
2381	    \qquad
2382	    f' \;=\; e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'} .
2383	  \]
2384	  The inner composite \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) is the four-factor map
2385	  \[
2386	    f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr) \;\circ\;
2387	    (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g', f})^{\mathrm{hom}} \;\circ\;
2388	    (\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}} \;\circ\;
2389	    (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f', g})^{\mathrm{inv}},
2390	  \]
2391	  with \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\), \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\),
2392	  \(\eta^{g'}\) the unit of the \((g')\)-adjunction, and
2393	  \(w : g' \circ f = f' \circ g\) the commutativity of the square.
2394	
2395	  \emph{Why a direct substitution fails.} The identity cannot be obtained by substituting the
2396	  leg factorizations directly, because the legs \(g'\) and \(f'\) appear in multiple positions
2397	  simultaneously: as the index of the adjunction \(\eta^{g'}\), inside the equality proof
2398	  \(w\) parametrizing \(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w\), inside the argument
2399	  \(f'_{*}\bigl((g')^{*}\widetilde M\bigr)\) of the codomain comparison

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}

### 1. `lem:modules_annihilator_ideal` (Picard_QuotScheme.tex, line 2414)
